page.title: How to Hire a CTO in the UK
page.intro:
Hiring the right CTO is one of the most critical and most difficult appointments in the technology-driven economy. The role spans technical architecture, engineering leadership, product strategy, and commercial awareness — and the combination of all four at a senior level is genuinely rare.

The CTO profile has bifurcated significantly in recent years. In early-stage businesses, the CTO is often a hands-on architect who codes alongside the engineering team and builds the technical foundation. In scaled businesses, the CTO is primarily an engineering leader and strategic technology advisor — the hands-on technical work is done by others. These are different roles and require different people.

The UK technology market is a candidate's market at senior levels. The best CTOs receive multiple approaches each year, have strong equity positions in their current organisations, and can afford to be selective. Reaching them requires a headhunter with credibility in the technology community and the ability to present an opportunity compellingly.
considerations: Distinguish clearly between a Builder CTO (hands-on, early-stage) and a Scaler CTO (process-oriented, engineering leadership focus) — these profiles do not overlap.
Be honest about your tech stack and the technical debt the incoming CTO will inherit — managing this conversation poorly destroys candidate trust early in the process.
Assess whether technical depth or team leadership is the primary requirement — trying to find both at the highest level significantly narrows the pool.
Benchmark compensation carefully — equity and total package competitiveness is the primary lever in CTO hiring, not job title or role description.
Remote and hybrid expectations must be clear from the outset — location flexibility is a major differentiator in technology hiring.
page.salaryRange: £85,000–£340,000+ depending on stage, sector, and equity component
page.timeline: 10–16 weeks from briefing to accepted offer
page.whySearch: Technology leadership is a candidate's market. The best CTOs can choose their next role and are in no hurry. A headhunter who is embedded in the technology community — knows the ecosystem, attends the conferences, understands the equity benchmarks — can identify candidates that are genuinely right for the stage and context, and approach them with credibility.
